paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## NEURAL TIME-DEPENDENT PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a crucial role in studying a vast number of problems in science and engineering. Numerically solving nonlinear and/or highdimensional PDEs is frequently a challenging task. Inspired by the traditional finite difference and finite elements methods and emerging advancements in machine learning, we propose a sequence-to-sequence learning (Seq2Seq) framework called Neural-PDE, which allows one to automatically learn governing rules of any timedependent PDE system from existing data by using a bidirectional LSTM encoder, and predict the solutions in next _n_ time steps. One critical feature of our proposed framework is that the Neural-PDE is able to simultaneously learn and simulate all variables of interest in a PDE system. We test the Neural-PDE by a range of examples, from one-dimensional PDEs to a multi-dimensional and nonlinear complex fluids model. The results show that the Neural-PDE is capable of learning the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators defining the initial-boundary-value problem of a PDE system without the knowledge of the specific form of the PDE system. In our experiments, the Neural-PDE can efficiently extract the dynamics within 20 epochs training and produce accurate predictions. Furthermore, unlike the traditional machine learning approaches for learning PDEs, such as CNN and MLP, which require great quantity of parameters for model precision, the Neural-PDE shares parameters among all time steps, and thus considerably reduces computational complexity and leads to a fast learning algorithm.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The research of time-dependent partial differential equations (PDEs) is regarded as one of the most important disciplines in applied mathematics. PDEs appear ubiquitously in a broad spectrum of fields including physics, biology, chemistry, and finance, to name a few. Despite their fundamental importance, most PDEs can not be solved analytically and have to rely on numerical solving methods. Developing efficient and accurate numerical schemes for solving PDEs, therefore, has been an active research area over the past few decades (Courant et al., 1967; Osher & Sethian, 1988; LeVeque; Cockburn et al., 2012; Thomas, 2013; Johnson, 2012). Still, devising stable and accurate schemes with acceptable computational cost is a difficult task, especially when nonlinear and(or) high-dimensional PDEs are considered. Additionally, PDE models emerged from science and engineering disciplines usually require huge empirical data for model calibration and validation, and determining the multidimensional parameters in such a PDE system poses another challenge (Peng et al., 2020).

Deep learning is considered to be the state-of-the-art tool in classification and prediction of nonlinear inputs, such as image, text, and speech (Litjens et al., 2017; Devlin et al., 2018; LeCun et al., 1998; Krizhevsky et al., 2012; Hinton et al., 2012). Recently, considerable efforts have been made to employ deep learning tools in designing data-driven methods for solving PDEs (Han et al., 2018; Long et al., 2018; Sirignano & Spiliopoulos, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019). Most of these approaches are based on fully-connected neural networks (FCNNs), convolutional neural networks(CNNs) and multilayer perceptron (MLP). These neural network structures usually require an increment of the layers to improve the predictive accuracy (Raissi et al., 2019), and subsequently lead to a more complicated model due to the additional parameters. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are one type of neural network architectures. RNNs predict the next time step value by using the input data from the current

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

and previous states and share parameters across all inputs. This idea (Sherstinsky, 2020) of using current and previous step states to calculate the state at the next time step is not unique to RNNs. In fact, it is ubiquitously used in numerical PDEs. Almost all time-stepping numerical methods applied to solve time-dependent PDEs, such as Euler’s, Crank-Nicolson, high-order Taylor and its variance Runge-Kutta (Ascher et al., 1997) time-stepping methods, update numerical solution by utilizing solution from previous steps.

This motivates us to think what would happen if we replace the previous step data in the neural network with numerical solution data to PDE supported on grids. It is possible that the neural network behaves like a time-stepping method, for example, forward Euler’s method yields the numerical solution at a new time point as the current state output (Chen et al., 2018). Since the numerical solution on each of the grid point (for finite difference) or grid cell (for finite element) computed at a set of contiguous time points can be treated as neural network input in the form of one time sequence of data, the deep learning framework can be trained to predict any time-dependent PDEs from the time series data supported on some grids if the bidirectional structure is applied (Huang et al., 2015; Schuster & Paliwal, 1997). In other words, the supervised training process can be regarded as a practice of the deep learning framework to learn the numerical solution from the input data, by learning the coefficients on neural network layers.

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) is a neural network built upon RNNs. Unlike vanilla RNNs, which suffer from losing long term information and high probability of gradient vanishing or exploding, LSTM has a specifically designed memory cell with a set of new gates such as input gate and forget gate. Equipped with these new gates which control the time to preserve and pass the information, LSTM is capable of learning long term dependencies without the danger of having gradient vanishing or exploding. In the past two decades, LSTM has been widely used in the field of natural language processing (NLP), such as machine translation, dialogue systems, question answering systems (Lipton et al., 2015).

Inspired by numerical PDE schemes and LSTM neural network, we propose a new deep learning framework, denoted as Neural-PDE. It simulates multi-dimensional governing laws, represented by time-dependent PDEs, from time series data generated on some grids and predicts the next _n_ time steps data. The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs. The detailed structures of the Neural-PDE and data normalization are introduced in Section 3.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 briefly reviews finite difference method for solving PDEs. Section 3 contains detailed description of designing the Neural-PDE. In Section 4 and Appendix A of the paper, we apply the Neural-PDE to solve four different PDEs, including the 1-dimensional(1D) wave equation, the 2-dimensional(2D) heat equation, and two systems of PDEs: the invicid Burgers’ equations and a coupled Navier Stokes-Cahn Hilliard equations, which widely appear in multiscale modeling of complex fluid systems. We demonstrate the robustness of the Neural-PDE, which achieves convergence within 20 epochs with an admissible mean squared error, even when we add Gaussian noise in the input data.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

2.1 TIME DEPENDENT PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

A time-dependent partial differential equation is an equation of the form:

where _u_ = _u_ ( _t, x_ 1 _, ..., xn_ ) is known, _xi ∈_ R are spatial variables, and the operator _f_ maps R _�→_ R. For example, consider the parabolic heat equation: _ut_ = _α_[2] ∆ _u_ , where _u_ represents the temperature and _f_ is the Laplacian operator ∆. Eq. (2.1.1) can be solved by finite difference methods, which is briefly reviewed below for the self-completeness of the paper.

## 2.2 FINITE DIFFERENCE METHOD

Consider using a finite difference method (FDM) to solve a two-dimensional second-order PDE of the form:
_ut_ = _f_ ( _x, y, ux, uy, uxx, uyy_ ) _,_ ( _x, y_ ) _∈_ Ω _⊂_ R[2] _, t ∈_ R[+] _∪{_ 0 _} ,_ (2.2.1) with some proper boundary conditions. Let Ω be Ω= [ _xa, xb_ ] _×_ [ _ya, yb_ ], and

where _tn_ = _nδt,_ 0 _≤ n ≤ N_ , and _δt_ = _N[T]_[for] _[t][ ∈]_[[0] _[, T]_[]][, and some large integer] _[ N]_[.] _[x][i]_[=] _[ iδx,]_[0] _[ ≤] i ≤ Nx_ , _δx_ = _[x][a] N[−] x[x][b]_ for _x ∈_ [ _xa, xb_ ]. _yj_ = _jδy,_ 0 _≤ j ≤ Ny_ , _δy_ = _[y][a] N[−] y[y][b]_ for _y ∈_ [ _ya, yb_ ]. _Nx_ and _Ny_ are integers.

The central difference method approximates the spatial derivatives as follows (Thomas, 2013):

To this end, the explicit time-stepping scheme to update next step solution _u[n]_[+1] is given by:

Apparently, the finite difference method (2.2.7) for updating _u[n]_[+1] on a grid point relies on the previous time steps’ solutions, supported on the grid point and its neighbours. The scheme (2.2.7) updates _u[n] i,j_[+1] using four points of _u[n]_ values (see Figure 1). Similarly, the finite element method (FEM) approximates the new solution by calculating the corresponded mesh cell coefficient (see Appendix), which is updated by its related nearby coefficients on the mesh. From this perspective, one may regard the numerical schemes for solving time-dependent PDEs as methods catching the information from neighbourhood data of interest.

## 3.1 MATHEMATICAL MOTIVATION

Recurrent neural network including LSTM is an artificial neural network structure of the form (Lipton et al., 2015):

where _**x**[t] ∈_ R _[d]_ is the input data of the _t[th]_ state and _**h**[t][−]_[1] _∈_ R _[h]_ denotes the processed value in its previous state by the hidden layers. The output _**y**[t]_ of the current state is updated by the current state value _**h**[t]_ :

Here **W** _[hx] ∈_ R _[h][×][d]_ , **W** _[hh] ∈_ R _[h][×][h]_ , **W** _[hy] ∈_ R _[h][×][h]_ are the matrix of weights, vectors _**b** h,_ _**b** y ∈_ R _[h]_ are the coefficients of bias, and _σ, σa, σb, σc, σd_ are corresponded activation and mapping functions.

With proper design of input and forget gate, LSTM can effectively yield a better control over the gradient flow and better preserve useful information from long-range dependencies (Graves & Schmidhuber, 2005).
Now consider a temporally continuous vector function _**u** ∈_ R _[n]_ given by an ordinary differential equation with the form:

Let _**u**[n]_ = _**u**_ ( _t_ = _nδt_ ), a forward Euler’s method for solving _**u**_ can be easily derived from the Taylor’s theorem which gives the following first-order accurate approximation of the time derivative:

Then we have:

Here _**u**_ ˆ _[n] ≈_ _**u**_ ( _nδt_ ) is the numerical approximation and _f_ 1 _≡_ _**u**[n]_ + _δt g_ ( _**u**[n]_ ) : R _[n] →_ R _[n]_ . Combining equations (3.1.1) and (3.1.6) one may notice that the residual networks, recurrent neural network and also LSTM networks can be regarded as a numerical scheme for solving time-dependent differential equations if more layers are added and smaller time steps are taken. (Chen et al., 2018)

Canonical structure for such recurrent neural network usually calculate the current state value by its previous time step value _**h**[t][−]_[1] and current state input _**x**[t]_ . Similarly, in numerical PDEs, the next step data at a grid point is updated from the previous (and current) values on its nearby grid points (see Eq. 2.2.7).
Thus, what if we replace the temporal input _**h**[t][−]_[1] and _**x**[t]_ with spatial information? A simple sketch of the upwinding method for a 1 _d_ example of _u_ ( _x, t_ ):

will be:

The function _fθ,η_ simulates the dynamics of the Neural-PDE with paramaters _θ_ and _η_ . By applying Bidirectional neural network, all grid data are transferred and it enables LSTM to simulate the PDEs as :

For a time-dependent PDE, if we map all our grid data into an input matrix which contains the information of _δx, δt_ , then the neural network would regress such coefficients as constants and will learn and filter the physical rules from all the _k_ mesh grids data as:

The LSTM neural network is designed to overcome the vanishing gradient issue through hidden layers, therefore we use such recurrent structure to increase the stability of the numerical approach in deep learning. The highly nonlinear function _fθ,η_ simulates the dynamics of updating rules for _u[n] i_[+1] , which works in a way similar to a finite difference method (section 2.2) or a finite element method.

## 3.2 NEURAL-PDE

In particular, we use the bidirectional LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997; Graves & Schmidhuber, 2005) to better retain the state information from data on grid points which are neighbourhoods in the mesh but far away in input matrix.

The right frame of Figure 3 shows the overall design of the Neural-PDE. Denote the time series data at collocation points as _**a**[N]_ 1 _[,]_ _**[ a]**[N]_ 2 _[,][ · · ·][,]_ _**[ a]**[N] k_ with _**a**[N] i_ = [ˆ _u_[0] _i[,]_[ ˆ] _[u]_[1] _i[,][ · · ·][,]_[ ˆ] _[u][N] i_[]][at] _[i][th]_[point.][The][superscript][represents] different time points. ## 3.3 DATA INITIALIZATION AND GRID POINT RESHAPE

In order to feed data into our sequence model framework, we map the PDE solution data onto a _K × N_ matrix, where _K ∈_ Z[+] is the dimension of the grid points and _N ∈_ Z[+] is the length of the time series data on each grid point. There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE. For example, a 2 _d_

## 4 COMPUTER EXPERIMENTS

Since the Neural-PDE is a sequence to sequence learning framework which allows one to predict within any time period by the given data. One may test the Neural-PDE using different permutations of training and predicting time periods for its efficiency, robustness and accuracy. In the following examples, the whole dataset is randomly splitted in 80% for traning and 20% for testing. We will predict the next _tp ∈_ [31 _× δt,_ 40 _× δt_ ] PDE solution by using its previous _ttr ∈_ [0 _,_ 30 _× δt_ ] data as:

Table 1 summaries the experimental results of the Neural-PDE model on 4 different PDEs, which achieve extremely small MSEs from _∼_ 10 _[−]_[5] to _∼_ 10 _[−]_[7] . Table 2 shows the comparison results of our proposed Neural-PDE with the state-of-the-art method Physically Informed Artificial Neural Networks (PINN) (Raissi et al., 2019) on two PDEs (1 _d_ Allen-Cahn and 1 _d_ Burgers’ equation). Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).

Figure 4: Neural-PDE shows ideal prediction on Burgers’ equation.

## EXAMPLE: INVISCID BURGERS’ EQUATION
Inviscid Burgers’ equation is a classical nonlinear PDE in fluid dynamics. In this example, we consider an invicid Burgers’ equation which has the following hyperbolic form:

and with initial and boundary conditions:

The invicid Burgers’ equation is hard to deal with in numerical PDEs due to the discontinuities (shock waves) in the solutions. We use a upwinding finite difference scheme to create the training data and put the velocity _u, v_ in to the input matrix. Let _δx_ = _δy_ = 10 _[−]_[2] _, δt_ = 10 _[−]_[3] , our empirical results (see Figure 4) show that the Neural-PDE is able to learn the shock waves, boundary conditions and the rules of the equation, and predict _u_ and _v_ simultaneously with an overall MSE of 1 _._ 4018 _×_ 10 _[−]_[5] . The heat maps of exact solution and predicted solution are shown in Figure 5.

## EXAMPLE: MULTISCALE MODELING: COUPLED CAHN–HILLIARD–NAVIER–STOKES SYSTEM

Finally, let’s consider the following 2 _d_ Cahn–Hilliard–Navier–Stokes system widely used for modeling complex fluids:

In this complicated example we will use the following initial condition:

This fluid system can be derived by the energetic variational approach (Forster, 2013). The complex fluids system has the following features: the micro-structures such as the molecular configurations, the interaction between different scales and the competition between multi-phase fluids (Hyon et al., 2010). Here _**u**_ is the velocity and _φ_ ( _x, y, t_ ) _∈_ [0 _,_ 1] denotes the volume fraction of one fluid phase. _M_ is the diffusion coefficient and _µ_ is the chemical potential of _φ_ . Equation (4.0.10) indicates the incompressibility of the fluid. Solving such PDE system is notorious because of its high nonlinearity and multi-physical and coupled features. One may use the decoupled projection method (Guermond et al., 2006) to numerically solve it efficiently or an implicit method which however is computationally

expensive. Another challenge of deep learning in solving a system like this is how to process the data to improve the learning efficiency when the input matrix consists of variables such as _φ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] with large magnitude value and variable of very small values such as _p ∼_ 10 _[−]_[5] . For the Neural-PDE to better extract and learn the physical features of variables in different scales, we normalized the _p_ data with a _sigmoid_ function. Set _δt_ = 5 _×_ 10 _[−]_[4] , and here the training dataset is generated by a FEM solver FreeFem++ (Hecht, 2012) using a Crank-Nicolson finite element scheme. Our Neural-PDE prediction shows that the physical features of _p_ and _φ_ have been successfully captured with an overall MSE: 6 _._ 1631 _×_ 10 _[−]_[7] (see Figure 7).

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we proposed a novel sequence recurrent deep learning framework: Neural-PDE, which is capable of intelligently filtering and learning solutions of time-dependent PDEs. One key innovation of our method is that the time marching method from the numerical PDEs is applied in the deep learning framework, and the neural network is trained to explore the accurate numerical solutions for prediction.

The state-of-the-art researches have shown the promising power of deep learning in solving highdimensional nonlinear problems in engineering, biology and finance with efficiency in computation and accuracy in prediction. However, there are still unresolved issues in applying deep learning in PDEs. For instance, the stability and convergence of the numerical algorithms have been rigorously studied by applied mathematicians. Due to the high nonlinearity of the neural network system and the curse of dimensionality (Hutzenthaler et al., 2019), theorems guiding stability and convergence of solutions predicted by the neural network are to be revealed.

Lastly, it would be helpful and interesting if one can theoretically characterize a numerical scheme from the neural network coefficients and learn the forms or mechanics from the scheme and prediction. We leave these questions for further study.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.label: Zero Shot (claude-opus-4.7)
zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a sequence-to-sequence LSTM-based framework (Neural-PDE) for learning time-dependent PDE solutions from data. The motivation — drawing parallels between time-stepping numerical schemes and recurrent networks — is reasonable, and the empirical results on several PDEs look promising. However, the paper suffers from several technical and expositional weaknesses: key equations and derivations are partially cut off or formatted as placeholders, the mathematical motivation section mixes temporal and spatial indexing in a confusing way, the comparison with PINN (the sole baseline) is quite superficial, and the method description in Section 3.2 is too terse for reproduction. Several parameter and indexing inconsistencies also undermine clarity. The work is interesting but needs significant technical tightening and more rigorous comparisons.

comments.title: Mathematical motivation conflates temporal and spatial recursion without justification
comments.quote: Thus, what if we replace the temporal input _**h**[t][−]_[1] and _**x**[t]_ with spatial information?
comments.explanation: The authors attempt to justify using an RNN/LSTM structure by paralleling forward Euler and an upwinding spatial scheme. But the forward Euler analogy in (3.1.6) uses time recursion u^{n+1}=u^n+δt·g(u^n), while the upwinding analogy (3.1.7-3.1.8) replaces the temporal previous state h^{t-1} with the spatial neighbor h_{i-1}, which is a fundamentally different operation. The paper then claims 'the neural network could be seen as a forward Euler method for equation 3.1.7,' but this conflates spatial upwinding (which is a spatial derivative approximation) with temporal Euler integration. The resulting interpretation that the LSTM cell 'acts like' a time-stepping scheme for the PDE is not made rigorous — it is a loose analogy presented as motivation. For a careful reader this is misleading.

comments.title: Inconsistent weight matrix dimension for Why
comments.explanation: The standard dimension for the hidden-to-output weight matrix W^{hy} should be R^{o×h} where o is the output dimension, not R^{h×h}. Given that y^t is the output of the current state (typically of different dimension than h), W^{hy} ∈ R^{h×h} is only correct by coincidence if output and hidden dimensions match. Also, the bias b_y ∈ R^h is stated as having dimension h, consistent with this assumption, but the paper never clarifies this constraint. This is a minor notational slip but would confuse a careful reader.

comments.title: Baseline comparison with PINN is inadequately specified
comments.quote: Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).
comments.explanation: The comparison claim is problematic because PINN is a fundamentally different framework: it is trained to satisfy the PDE residual with physics-informed loss and typically does not require ground-truth solution data at interior points, whereas Neural-PDE is a data-driven supervised method trained on pre-computed solutions from traditional solvers. Comparing test MSE without matching the training data/conditions, and claiming to 'outperform' PINN based on parameter count alone, is not a fair comparison. Additionally, the prediction horizon (30 steps input, 10 steps output) is quite short and doesn't demonstrate long-term predictive stability. The paper doesn't discuss whether PINN was trained with the same data/loss and doesn't quantify the MSE values for the comparison in a convincing way.

comments.title: Method description insufficient for reproduction
comments.quote: One then decoder, another bi-lstm with a dense layer:
comments.explanation: Section 3.2 describes the Neural-PDE architecture in only a few lines. Critical details are missing: (1) how exactly the encoder's output h_i is fed to the decoder (is it a context vector, per-step hidden state, or concatenation?); (2) the loss function is described as MSE but the prediction horizon structure (generating M future steps from N input steps) is not clear — is it autoregressive or one-shot? (3) activation functions in the dense layer; (4) how sigmoid normalization is applied to the pressure p in the Cahn-Hilliard example and whether normalization affects loss computation; (5) optimizer, learning rate, batch size, etc. A reader cannot reproduce this framework from the paper alone.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for grid spacing
comments.explanation: The formula as printed has x_a − x_b in the numerator, which would give a negative spacing since x_a < x_b. The intended formula should clearly be δx = (x_b − x_a)/N_x. This is likely an OCR artifact, but the same pattern appears for δy. Readers following the numerical scheme literally would be confused. Worth flagging only because the sign affects the update (2.2.7).

comments.title: Ambiguity about generalization across initial conditions
comments.quote: the Neural-PDE is capable of learning the initial conditions, boundary conditions and differential operators defining the initial-boundary-value problem of a PDE system without the knowledge of the specific form of the PDE system.
comments.explanation: This overstates the claim. The Neural-PDE is trained on solutions from specific initial/boundary conditions and appears to be tested on the same trajectory (80%/20% split). The paper does not demonstrate generalization to new initial conditions, new boundary conditions, or new physical parameters — which would be required to justify saying it 'learns' the operator defining the IBVP. The framework appears to be solving a time-series forecasting problem for a specific PDE realization, not learning a general PDE solver. This is a notable overclaim in the abstract and conclusion.

comments.title: Missing dimensional analysis in equation 3.1.10 claim
comments.quote: For a time-dependent PDE, if we map all our grid data into an input matrix which contains the information of _δx, δt_ , then the neural network would regress such coefficients as constants and will learn and filter the physical rules from all the _k_ mesh grids data as:
comments.explanation: The paper claims the network will 'regress such coefficients as constants' but provides no justification or demonstration that the network actually learns grid-spacing coefficients. Since δx and δt are fixed during training (generated on a single grid), the network cannot distinguish between a solution on one grid vs. another. The claim that Neural-PDE would work with different δx, δt values is not supported. Evaluation at different grid resolutions is not shown.

comments.title: Presence of parameter definitions without full context
comments.explanation: The upwinding scheme update (3.1.8) uses a coefficient ν (wave speed?) that has not been previously defined in the paper. The equation 3.1.7 as shown is incomplete/truncated, so the reader cannot verify whether ν is the transport velocity, and the upwinding scheme requires ν>0 for correct upwind direction. This is a gap in the mathematical setup.

zero_shot__gpt-5.5.label: Zero Shot (gpt-5.5)
zero_shot__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper has an appealing high-level idea—treating gridded PDE solution histories as sequences and using a bidirectional LSTM encoder-decoder to predict future states—and the examples suggest that the architecture can interpolate short-time trajectories generated by standard solvers. However, several central claims are stronger than what the method description and experiments support. The most serious concerns are methodological: the train/test protocol appears to split a single generated trajectory randomly rather than testing on genuinely unseen initial/boundary conditions or PDE instances, the ordering of multidimensional grid data is asserted to be unimportant despite the order-sensitive nature of BiLSTMs, and the paper claims general accuracy or applicability to “any” time-dependent PDE without stability, consistency, or generalization evidence. There are also localized technical and reproducibility issues in the PDE preliminaries, finite-difference discussion, baseline comparison, and data normalization.

comments.title: The PDE solution is described as known rather than unknown
comments.quote: where _u_ = _u_ ( _t, x_ 1 _, ..., xn_ ) is known, _xi ∈_ R are spatial variables, and the operator _f_ maps R _→_ R.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a standard definition of a time-dependent PDE initial-boundary-value problem, where the goal is to solve for an unknown function u. In that context, saying that u is “known” is mathematically backwards: the operator, domain, initial data, and boundary data may be known, but u is the unknown solution. The surrounding discussion confirms that the authors are indeed discussing numerical solution of PDEs, so this is likely a wording error rather than a conceptual misunderstanding. Still, in a preliminaries section this is a real inconsistency because it reverses the role of the main object being solved for.

comments.title: Finite-difference stencil is described as using four points although the scheme includes the center point
comments.quote: The scheme (2.2.7) updates _u[n] i,j_[+1] using four points of _u[n]_ values (see Figure 1).
comments.explanation: The authors are correctly emphasizing that finite-difference updates use local neighborhood information, which is the relevant intuition for their neural architecture. However, for a standard two-dimensional central-difference discretization of a second-order PDE with first and second spatial derivatives, the update at \((i,j)\) generally depends on the center value \(u^n_{i,j}\) plus its four axis-aligned neighbors \(u^n_{i\pm1,j}\), \(u^n_{i,j\pm1}\), i.e. a five-point stencil. Figure 1 also appears to show the center point. If the authors intended “four neighboring points,” the prose should say that; as written, it understates the stencil and conflicts with the usual finite-difference formula.

comments.title: Claim that input grid ordering needs no regularization is not justified for a BiLSTM
comments.quote: There is no regularization for the input order of the grid points data in the matrix because of the bi-directional structure of the Neural-PDE.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: because a bidirectional LSTM processes a sequence in both directions, they want it to propagate information from grid points that are distant in a flattened ordering. But bidirectionality does not make an LSTM permutation-invariant or insensitive to ordering. A BiLSTM still imposes a one-dimensional sequential adjacency structure; changing the flattening of a 2D or higher-dimensional mesh changes which grid points are near in the sequence and therefore changes the inductive bias and the effective information path length. This is especially important because the paper motivates the method by analogy to finite-difference and finite-element locality. The method description does not specify a canonical flattening, space-filling curve, graph traversal, or test of permutation sensitivity, so a reader could reasonably reach the incorrect conclusion that arbitrary ordering is safe. This affects reproducibility and the validity of the spatial-neighborhood argument.

comments.title: Random train/test split appears to test interpolation within one generated trajectory, not general PDE learning
comments.quote: Since the Neural-PDE is a sequence to sequence learning framework which allows one to predict within any time period by the given data. One may test the Neural-PDE using different permutations of training and predicting time periods for its efficiency, robustness and accuracy. In the following examples, the whole dataset is randomly splitted in 80% for traning and 20% for testing. We will predict the next _tp ∈_ [31 _× δt,_ 40 _× δt_ ] PDE solution by using its previous _ttr ∈_ [0 _,_ 30 _× δt_ ] data as:
comments.explanation: The paper's broader claims are about learning governing rules, boundary conditions, and differential operators from data. I therefore looked for whether the experiments test generalization to unseen initial conditions, boundary conditions, parameter values, forcing terms, or independent trajectories. This paragraph instead suggests that the “whole dataset” is randomly split 80/20 while each sample consists of early-time data used to predict nearby future-time data. If the split is over grid points or windows from the same numerical trajectory, then the test error measures interpolation/reconstruction on held-out pieces of a single solution field, not whether the model has learned a PDE operator in the sense used by numerical analysis or operator learning. The phrase “different permutations of training and predicting time periods” also does not specify exactly what constitutes an independent test sample. This ambiguity is central because it can make the reported very small MSEs look like evidence of general PDE learning when they may only reflect short-horizon interpolation on a fixed simulated trajectory.

comments.title: The paper overclaims guaranteed accuracy and applicability to any time-dependent PDE
comments.quote: The Neural-PDE is capable of intelligently processing related data from all spatial grids by using the bidirectional (Schuster & Paliwal, 1997) neural network, and thus guarantees the accuracy of the numerical solution and the feasibility in learning any time-dependent PDEs.
comments.explanation: The intuition that a bidirectional recurrent architecture can aggregate information across a flattened spatial grid is reasonable, and the paper gives several empirical examples. But the word “guarantees” is much stronger than what is established. A BiLSTM architecture by itself gives no numerical-analysis guarantee of consistency, stability, convergence, conservation, shock admissibility, boundary-condition satisfaction, or long-time accuracy. Similarly, “any time-dependent PDEs” is not supported by the experiments, which cover a small set of generated examples under particular discretizations and short prediction horizons. The conclusion later acknowledges that stability and convergence theory for neural predictions remains unresolved, which further conflicts with this guarantee. The claim should be weakened to an empirical capability observed on the tested problems.

comments.title: Baseline comparison with PINN is underspecified and may not be comparable
comments.quote: Table 2 shows the comparison results of our proposed Neural-PDE with the state-of-the-art method Physically Informed Artificial Neural Networks (PINN) (Raissi et al., 2019) on two PDEs (1 _d_ Allen-Cahn and 1 _d_ Burgers’ equation). Neural-PDE is able to outperform PINN while having much less parameters, where PINN contains 4 hidden layers with 200 neurons per layer and Neural-PDE only consists of 3 layers (2 bi-lstm with 20 neurons per layer and 1 dense output layer with 10 neurons).
comments.explanation: It is useful that the authors compare against a recognized baseline, and parameter efficiency is a legitimate consideration. However, the comparison is not sufficiently specified to support the claim that Neural-PDE “outperforms” PINN. PINNs and sequence-to-sequence predictors are typically trained under different information regimes: PINNs use collocation residuals and boundary/initial constraints, whereas Neural-PDE here appears to be trained directly on solution snapshots generated by a numerical solver. The excerpt does not clarify whether both methods receive the same observations, whether PINN hyperparameters were tuned, what loss terms and collocation points were used, whether the same train/test regions and boundary/initial data were used, or whether the reported MSE is on normalized or physical variables. Without these details, the performance and parameter-count comparison is not reproducible and may conflate different problem settings rather than architecture quality.

comments.title: Normalization of very small pressure values using a sigmoid is underspecified and potentially distorts the target
comments.quote: Another challenge of deep learning in solving a system like this is how to process the data to improve the learning efficiency when the input matrix consists of variables such as _φ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1] with large magnitude value and variable of very small values such as _p ∼_ 10 _[−]_[5] . For the Neural-PDE to better extract and learn the physical features of variables in different scales, we normalized the _p_ data with a _sigmoid_ function.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify a real multiscale-learning issue: combining fields with very different magnitudes can make an unweighted MSE dominated by the larger-scale variable. But the proposed remedy is not described precisely enough to reproduce, and the stated use of a sigmoid raises a technical concern. If one directly applies \(\sigma(p)\) to values of order \(10^{-5}\), the result is approximately 0.5 with variations of only order \(10^{-6}\), which may compress rather than amplify physically meaningful pressure differences. Perhaps the authors first rescale or standardize p and then apply a sigmoid, or perhaps “sigmoid” refers to a learned output activation, but the text does not say. It is also unclear whether MSE is computed in normalized or physical units and how inverse normalization is handled in the plotted or reported pressure fields. Because the reported overall MSE for the fluid system combines variables on different scales, this missing detail affects interpretation of the claimed accuracy.
